concepts.title: Coplanar forces in equilibrium
concepts.conceptBody:
Coplanar Forces in Equilibrium 

When an object is in equilibrium under the action of a set of two or more coplanar forces, each of three factors which comprises the possible movement of the object must be zero, i.e., the object has

(i) no linear movement along any two mutually perpendicular directions ox and oy.

(ii) no rotation about any axis.

The set of forces must, therefore, be such that

(a) the algebraic sum of the components parallel to ox is zero or $\text{ }\!\!\Sigma\!\!\text{ }{{\text{F}}_{\text{x}}}=0$

(b) the algebraic sum of the components parallel to oy is zero or $\text{ }\!\!\Sigma\!\!\text{ }{{\text{F}}_{\text{y}}}=0$

(c) the resultant moment about any specified axis is zero or $\text{ }\!\!\Sigma\!\!\text{ }{{\tau }_{\text{anyaxis }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}=0$

Thus, for the equilibrium of a set of two or more coplanar forces :

$\Rightarrow \begin{matrix} \sum ~{{\text{F}}_{\text{x}}}=0 \\ \sum ~{{\text{F}}_{\text{y}}}=0\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ and }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\sum ~{{\tau }_{\text{any }\!\!~\!\!\text{ axis }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}=0 \\ \end{matrix}$

Using the above three conditions, we get only three set of equations. So, in a problem number of unknowns should not be more than three.

concepts.title: Concurrent coplanar forces in equilibrium
concepts.conceptBody:
Equilibrium of Concurrent Coplanar Forces 

If an object is in equilibrium under two or more concurrent coplanar forces the algebraic sum of the components of forces in any two mutually perpendicular directions ox and oy should be zero, i.e., the set of forces must be such that :

(a) the algebraic sum of the components parallel to ox is zero, i.e., $\sum {{\text{F}}_{\text{x}}}=0$

(b) the algebraic sum of the components parallel to oy is zero, i.e., $\sum {{\text{F}}_{\text{y}}}=0$

Thus, for the equilibrium of two or more concurrent coplanar forces :

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & ~\sum ~{{\text{F}}_{\text{x}}}=0 \\ {} & ~\sum ~{{\text{F}}_{\text{y}}}=0 \\ \end{array}$

The third condition of zero moment about any specified axis is automatically satisfied if the moment is taken about the point of intersection of the forces. So, here we get only two equations. Thus, number of unknown in any problem should not be more than two.

concepts.title: Condition for equilibrium
concepts.conceptBody:
Lami's Theorem 

Lami's theorem in statics states that

if three coplanar forces are acting on a same point and keep it stationary, then it obeys the relation

$\frac{\text{A}}{\text{sin}\left( \gamma \right)}=\frac{\text{B}}{\text{sin}\left( \beta \right)}=\frac{\text{C}}{\text{sin}\left( \alpha \right)}$

where A, B and C are the magnitude of forces acting at the point (say P), and the values of $\alpha ,\beta $, and $\gamma $ are the angles directly opposite to the forces C, B and A respectively.

Lami's theorem is applied in static analysis of mechanical and structural systems.

concepts.title: Free Body Diagram
concepts.conceptBody:
Free Body Diagram

A free body diagram (FBD) consists of a diagrammatic representation of a single body or a sub-system of bodies isolated from its surroundings showing all the forces acting on it.

Consider, for example, a book lying on a horizontal surface.

A free body diagram of the book alone would consists of its weight 
 


$(\text{W}=\text{mg}$ ), acting through the centre of gravity and the reaction $\left( \text{N} \right)$ exerted on the book by the surface.

Example : Draw the FBD in following cases : 

Solution : 

Dumb Question 

(1) Why is the normal acting like this ? 

Ans. The direction of the normal force is always perpendicular to the surface of contact.

(2) Why is the tension direction like this? 

Ans. The direction of tension is always away from the point of contact.

Example : B is pulling A. If we need to draw FBD for both then?

concepts.title: Force between blocks pushed from one side
concepts.conceptBody:
Force between blocks pushed from one side

Example

Two blocks, ${{m}_{1}}~$= 3.0 kg are put in contact on a frictionless surface. A horizontal force F = 5.0 N is applied to one of them (see Fig. 1). (1) Find the force ${{F}_{a}}$ between the two blocks. (2) Find the force ${{F}_{b}}$ if the force F is applied to ${{m}_{1}}$ in the opposite direction. 

Solution : Fig. 1 is drawn in such a way that it suggests that the force $\text{F}$ is applied from the outside, but we know that this force can be considered as concentrated in the center of gravity of this block. We draw the Free Body Diagrams for these blocks. For the block ${{\text{m}}_{1}}$, it is on the left hand side of Fig. 2 , and for the block ${{\text{m}}_{2}}$ on the right hand side of this Figure.

 
 

The force are applied to the CG (center of gravity) of mass ${{m}_{1}}$ and ${{m}_{2}}$. In the explanations and calculations we will use bold face notation for vector quantities. It is easier to handle with text editors as opposed to handwriting, where arrows are more convenient to use. The ${{\text{N}}_{1}}$ force is a reaction force to the gravity force ${{\text{m}}_{1}}\text{ g}$ as described by Newton's third Law of Motion. Analogically, ${{\text{N}}_{2}}$ and ${{\text{m}}_{2}}\text{ g}$ are equal and opposite as this law requires.

For the ${{m}_{1}}$ block (the left part of Fig. 2) the direction of force $F$ is obvious from the text of the problem (see Fig. 1). But why is the force ${{\text{F}}_{\text{a}}}$ directed to the left ? Because this is the force extended on block ${{\text{m}}_{1}}$ by block ${{\text{m}}_{2}}$. Block ${{\text{m}}_{1}}$ "pushes" block ${{\text{m}}_{2}}$ so block ${{\text{m}}_{2}}$ creates an "opposite" force according to Newton's Third Law of Motion. Stop and think it over for a moment. This is a very crucial point of all problems involving Newton's Laws. Once you understand the idea of drawing the Free Body Diagram you will solve all problems involving mechanics much faster and more efficiently.

Now it's time to write equations based on Newton's Laws. In the vertical direction

${{\text{m}}_{1}}\text{ g}=\mathbf{N}$

so we can forget about these forces in further analysis.

In the horizontal direction the resultant force exerted on ${{\text{m}}_{1}}$ is $\mathbf{F}-{{\mathbf{F}}_{\text{a}}}$ and this is the force accelerating block $\text{m}$. Therefore we can write

$\mathbf{F}-{{\mathbf{F}}_{\text{a}}}={{\text{m}}_{1}}\text{a}$

The FBD for ${{\text{m}}_{2}}$ shows that the only the horizontal force acting on it is the exerted by block ${{\text{m}}_{1}}$. This force has a magnitude of the ${{\text{F}}_{\text{a}}}$ from the $\text{FBD}$ on the left side if Fig. 2, but the direction is opposite. This is the force accelerating block ${{\text{m}}_{2}}$. As both blocks are in contact they must have the same acceleration a. So, for the second block the equation of motion is

${{\text{F}}_{\text{a}}}={{\text{m}}_{2}}\text{a}$

We can drop out the vector notation from these two equations as the directions are well defined on the FBD's for both blocks.

From the (2) we have

$\text{a}={{\text{F}}_{\text{a}}}/{{\text{m}}_{2}}$

and substituting this acceleration into (1) we find, after a little elementary algebra,

${{\text{F}}_{\text{a}}}=\text{Fm}/2\left( {{\text{m}}_{1}}+{{\text{m}}_{2}} \right)$

And this is the answer to question (1) from the problem.

If the force $\text{F}$ is exerted from right to left, as in part (2) of the problem, the analogical reasoning will lead to the answer.

${{\text{F}}_{\text{B}}}=\text{F}{{\text{m}}_{1}}/\left( {{\text{m}}_{1}}+{{\text{m}}_{2}} \right)$

Substituting the values given in the problem we get

${{\text{F}}_{\text{a}}}=3.0\text{ N }\text{ and }{{\text{F}}_{\text{b}}}=1.2\text{ N}$

You can wonder why the force between the blocks is larger when you push from the left. This is because in that situation the block which is a kind of transmitter of force must push the larger mass $({{m}_{2}})$ than in the second situation, when the larger block is pushing the smaller one.

concepts.title: Psuedo force on an object in an elevator
concepts.conceptBody:
Psuedo Force

Suppose a block $\text{A}$ of mass $\text{m}$ is placed on a lift ascending with an acceleration ${{\text{a}}_{0}}$. Let $\text{N}$ be the normal reaction between the block and the floor of the lift. Free body diagram of $\text{A}$ in ground frame of reference (inertial) is shown in Fig.

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ N}-\text{mg}=\text{m}{{\text{a}}_{0}}$

or $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ N}=\text{m}\left( \text{g}+{{\text{a}}_{0}} \right)$ …(i)

But if we draw the free body diagram of A with respect to the elevator (a non-inertial frame of reference) without applying the pseudo force, we get

$\mathrm{N}^{\prime}-\mathrm{mg}=0$ or $\mathrm{N}^{\prime}=\mathrm{mg}$ …(ii)

Since, $\mathrm{N}^{\prime} \neq \mathrm{N}$, either of the equations is wrong. But if we apply a pseudo force in non-inertial frame of reference, $\mathrm{N}^{\prime}$ becomes equal to $\mathrm{N}$ as shown in Fig. 3.93. Acceleration of block with respect to elevator is zero. 
 

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ {N}'}-\text{mg}-\text{m}{{\text{a}}_{0}}=0$

or $\mathrm{N}^{\prime}=\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{g}+\mathrm{a}_0\right)$ (iii)

$\left. \therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ {N}'}=\text{N} \right]$

Pseudo force is given by$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}_{\mathrm{P}}=-\mathrm{m} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}_0$. Here, ${{\vec{a}}_{0}}$ is the acceleration of the non-inertial frame of reference and $m$ the mass of the body under consideration. In the whole chapter, we will show the pseudo force by $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}} \mathrm{P}$.

Thus, we may conclude that pseudo force is not a real force. When we draw the free body diagram of a mass, with respect to an inertial frame of reference we apply only the real forces (forces which are actually acting on the mass), but when the free body diagram is drawn from a non-inertial frame of reference a pseudo force (in addition to all real forces) has to be applied to make the equation $\mathbf{\vec{F}}=\text{m}\vec{a}$ to be valid in this frame also.

Note : In case of rotating frame of reference this pseudo force is called the centrifugal force when applied for centrifugal acceleration. Let us take few examples of pseudo forces.

concepts.title: Contraint due to rope
concepts.conceptBody:
Constraint Relation 

It is the equation showing the relation between the motion of different bodies of a system in which motion of one body is constrained by other bodies.

There are three approaches to derive the constraint equation.

Approach (1) 

If ${{\text{m}}_{2}}$ moves by a distance $\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }{{\text{x}}_{2}}$ down, the string or $\text{m}$, is stretched by $2\left( \text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }{{\text{x}}_{1}} \right)$

Since in this question one end is fixed, therefore $2\text{x}$ comes from string attached to ${{\text{m}}_{2}}$.

Note : If the end has not been fixed then the movement of $\text{m}$, will not be $2\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }{{\text{x}}_{2}}$, it will depend on the movement of the unfixed end also. This will be illustrated in the coming examples.

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }{{\text{x}}_{1}}=2\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }{{\text{x}}_{2}}$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{x}}_{1}}=2{{\text{x}}_{2}}$

differentiating it twice for getting relation in acceleration

${{\text{a}}_{1}}=2{{\text{a}}_{2}}$

Approach (2) 

This approach is based on the concept that the length of spring remains constant (assuming that string is inextensible) we calculate the length of string

i.e. $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{x}_{1}}+\left( {{x}_{2}}+{{x}_{2}} \right)+d=1$

where $d$ is a constant length, as will not change on the movement of any block & 1 is also constant.

differentiating the equation twice

$\left(-a_1\right)+2 a_2=0$

$a_1=2 a_2$

{ Note : derivative of decreasing length (such as ${{\text{x}}_{1}}$ in this question) is taken as negative, whereas for increasing length the derivative is positive

Approach (3) 

(Work-Energy Method)

Concept : Work done by the tension in string in all the frame of reference $=0$

Reason : String is inextensible

i.e. Elastic potential energy stored in string = 0

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ T}\left( -{{\text{x}}_{2}} \right)+\text{T}\left( {{\text{x}}_{1}} \right)=0$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{x}}_{1}}=2{{\text{x}}_{2}}$

$\Rightarrow a_1=2 a_2$

If $T$ and $T^{\prime}$ be the tensions in the two parts of the string, then
